text: Incident postmortem: Checkout latency after release v42. The affected service was checkout-api. Alerts included: P1: checkout-api p95 latency above 2400 ms for 8 minutes checkout-api container memory at 93 percent and rising payment-worker queue depth elevated but stable. Relevant logs included: 10:02 deploy release=checkout-api:v42 commit=9af31d2 actor=deploy-bot 10:04 warn heap_growth_mb=512 route=/cart/quote feature=recommendations 10:06 error oom_risk request_id=c-1842 tenant=retail-east 10:07 warn gc_pause_ms=821 route=/checkout feature=recommendations 10:09 info rollback_candidate release=checkout-api:v41 healthy_baseline=true 10:05 warn queue_depth=184 reason=upstream checkout-api latency 10:08 info retries within budget; no payment processor errors. Relevant metrics: checkout-api.latency_p95_ms moved from 180 to 3100; checkout-api.memory_percent moved from 48 to 95; checkout-api.error_rate moved from 0.01 to 0.17; payment-worker.queue_depth moved from 12 to 201; payment-worker.error_rate moved from 0.0 to 0.01. The root cause was bad_deploy_memory_leak. Safe remediation options were: rollback_checkout_api_v42, disable_checkout_recommendations_flag. Unsafe remediations to avoid were: restart_primary_database, purge_all_sessions. Stakeholder impact: Checkout is slow for a subset of retail-east users; payments are delayed but not failing.

Runbook for checkout-api: first inspect alerts, then check metrics for checkout-api, payment-worker. Query logs for terms such as checkout-api:v42, heap_growth_mb, memory, gc_pause_ms, recommendations, rollback_checkout_api_v42. Do not claim a root cause until evidence links symptoms to bad_deploy_memory_leak. Prefer rollback_checkout_api_v42 when the evidence matches this incident. Avoid red herrings such as payment-worker queue, retail-east tenant.

Stakeholder communication for Checkout latency after release v42: name the affected service, describe user impact, separate confirmed facts from hypotheses, mention the mitigation only after it is deployed, and set expectations for monitoring or the next update. For this incident, the impact was: Checkout is slow for a subset of retail-east users; payments are delayed but not failing.

Incident postmortem: Orders API saturation during inventory sync. The affected service was orders-api. Alerts included: P1: orders-api 5xx rate above 8 percent database connection wait time above 1200 ms inventory-sync retries climbing. Relevant logs included: 14:21 error db_pool_timeout pool=orders-main waited_ms=1500 14:22 warn active_connections=80 pool_max=80 endpoint=/orders/create 14:23 error request_failed reason=db_pool_timeout trace=o-771 14:25 info config_change inventory-sync batch_size=5000 14:20 info started full catalog reconciliation batch_size=5000 14:24 warn retrying order reservation due upstream orders-api 503. Relevant metrics: orders-api.error_rate moved from 0.01 to 0.12; orders-api.db_wait_ms moved from 80 to 1900; orders-api.cpu_percent moved from 42 to 62; inventory-sync.retry_count moved from 0 to 144; inventory-sync.throughput moved from 900 to 850. The root cause was db_connection_pool_exhaustion. Safe remediation options were: increase_orders_pool_and_restart_workers. Unsafe remediations to avoid were: drop_orders_read_replica, disable_all_inventory_sync. Stakeholder impact: New order creation is intermittently failing; inventory sync is a trigger, not the root fault.

Runbook for orders-api: first inspect alerts, then check metrics for inventory-sync, orders-api. Query logs for terms such as db_pool_timeout, active_connections=80, pool_max=80, db_wait_ms, increase_orders_pool_and_restart_workers. Do not claim a root cause until evidence links symptoms to db_connection_pool_exhaustion. Prefer increase_orders_pool_and_restart_workers when the evidence matches this incident. Avoid red herrings such as inventory-sync retries, catalog reconciliation.

Stakeholder communication for Orders API saturation during inventory sync: name the affected service, describe user impact, separate confirmed facts from hypotheses, mention the mitigation only after it is deployed, and set expectations for monitoring or the next update. For this incident, the impact was: New order creation is intermittently failing; inventory sync is a trigger, not the root fault.

Incident postmortem: Profile service cache stampede. The affected service was profile-service. Alerts included: P2: profile-service latency above 900 ms cache hit rate dropped below 35 percent user-feed showing elevated dependency latency. Relevant logs included: 09:41 config ttl_seconds=0 source=feature_flag profile-cache-expiry 09:43 warn cache_miss_rate=0.72 keyspace=user_profile 09:44 warn duplicate_rebuilds key=user:483 count=48 09:45 info dependency user-feed slow because profile-service latency 09:43 warn evictions normal memory_percent=57 09:45 info cluster healthy nodes=6. Relevant metrics: profile-service.latency_p95_ms moved from 180 to 1240; profile-service.cache_hit_rate moved from 0.93 to 0.25; profile-service.origin_qps moved from 400 to 3400; cache.memory_percent moved from 55 to 57; cache.evictions moved from 12 to 13. The root cause was cache_ttl_misconfiguration. Safe remediation options were: restore_profile_cache_ttl_singleflight, rate_limit_profile_rebuilds. Unsafe remediations to avoid were: flush_all_profile_cache, scale_down_cache_cluster. Stakeholder impact: Profile reads are slow and user-feed is degraded through dependency latency.

Runbook for profile-service: first inspect alerts, then check metrics for cache, profile-service. Query logs for terms such as ttl_seconds=0, cache_miss_rate, duplicate_rebuilds, cache_hit_rate, restore_profile_cache_ttl_singleflight. Do not claim a root cause until evidence links symptoms to cache_ttl_misconfiguration. Prefer restore_profile_cache_ttl_singleflight when the evidence matches this incident. Avoid red herrings such as cache evictions, user-feed latency.

Stakeholder communication for Profile service cache stampede: name the affected service, describe user impact, separate confirmed facts from hypotheses, mention the mitigation only after it is deployed, and set expectations for monitoring or the next update. For this incident, the impact was: Profile reads are slow and user-feed is degraded through dependency latency.

Incident postmortem: Auth service certificate expiry. The affected service was auth-service. Alerts included: P1: login success rate below 70 percent edge-gateway upstream TLS failures to auth-service support tickets mention intermittent login loops. Relevant logs included: 18:02 error upstream_tls_handshake_failed service=auth-service verify=certificate_expired 18:03 warn retrying upstream auth-service status=525 18:05 info canary route unaffected service=marketing-site 18:01 info cpu_percent=34 memory_percent=41 18:04 warn no application exceptions in auth handlers 18:06 info cert_subject=auth.internal expires_at=2026-04-25T12:00:00Z. Relevant metrics: auth-service.login_success_rate moved from 0.98 to 0.61; auth-service.cpu_percent moved from 30 to 35; auth-service.memory_percent moved from 39 to 42; edge-gateway.tls_failures moved from 0 to 420; edge-gateway.upstream_525 moved from 0 to 281. The root cause was expired_tls_certificate. Safe remediation options were: rotate_auth_service_certificate. Unsafe remediations to avoid were: disable_tls_verification_globally, restart_oauth_database. Stakeholder impact: Some users cannot log in because edge traffic cannot establish TLS to auth-service.

Runbook for auth-service: first inspect alerts, then check metrics for auth-service, edge-gateway. Query logs for terms such as upstream_tls_handshake_failed, certificate_expired, expires_at, tls_failures, rotate_auth_service_certificate. Do not claim a root cause until evidence links symptoms to expired_tls_certificate. Prefer rotate_auth_service_certificate when the evidence matches this incident. Avoid red herrings such as login loops, auth database.

Stakeholder communication for Auth service certificate expiry: name the affected service, describe user impact, separate confirmed facts from hypotheses, mention the mitigation only after it is deployed, and set expectations for monitoring or the next update. For this incident, the impact was: Some users cannot log in because edge traffic cannot establish TLS to auth-service.
